title: How Machines Learn вЂ” The Deep Theory
key_insight: Why does gradient descent on overparameterised networks find good solutions? This is the central mystery of deep learning theory вЂ” and it's not fully solved.
body:
## The Double Descent Phenomenon

Classical ML theory: adding parameters past the interpolation threshold increases test error (overfitting). More parameters = worse.

Deep learning reality:

```
Under-parameterised    Interpolation  Over-parameterised
     regime               threshold        regime

Test      /\                               \
error    /  \    в†ђ Classical ML           \  в†’ Deep Learning wins here
        /    \                              \
       /      \                              \_________
в”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв†’ parameters
```

When massively over-parameterised (more params than data points), test error falls again. Why? The network can fit training data while finding smooth solutions that generalise. This is the double descent phenomenon (Belkin et al., 2019).

## The Neural Tangent Kernel (NTK) Regime

For infinitely wide networks with tiny learning rates, gradient descent training is equivalent to kernel regression with a specific kernel (the NTK). This provides theoretical guarantees:

```python
# In the NTK regime (conceptually):
# Network behaviour в‰€ a linear model in a very high-dimensional feature space
# Training is convex in this space в†’ guaranteed convergence to global minimum

# BUT: real networks don't stay in the NTK regime
# Feature learning (weights that change their representation) is what makes
# deep learning powerful вЂ” and what NTK theory doesn't capture
```

## Grokking: Sudden Generalisation

```python
# Power et al. (2022): 'Grokking'
# Network perfectly memorises training data (100% train acc, near-chance test acc)
# Training continues past this point for thousands more steps
# Suddenly: test accuracy jumps to near-perfect
# The network discovered a general algorithm, not just memorisation

# Why? Weight decay forces the network to find compact solutions
# Eventually the compact solution that generalises is found
# Implications: don't stop training early; regularisation matters in subtle ways
```

## The Mechanistic Interpretability Frontier

New research direction (Anthropic, DeepMind, Neel Nanda): understand *what* networks have learned.

```python
# Superposition hypothesis: networks represent more features than they have neurons
# by encoding multiple features in superposed patterns

# Circuits research: individual attention heads perform specific, interpretable operations
# Example: 'Indirect Object Identification' circuit in GPT-2
# вЂ” specific heads copy subject tokens
# вЂ” specific heads suppress incorrect positions
# вЂ” the whole algorithm emerges from <10 attention heads
```

This field will redefine how we understand training вЂ” not as weight adjustment, but as algorithm discovery.
common_mistakes: Assuming validation loss plateau means training is done вЂ” grokking shows that continued training can suddenly produce generalisation
Treating loss as the only training signal вЂ” gradient norms, weight norms, and activation statistics all reveal training dynamics
Assuming smaller loss = better model вЂ” calibration (does confidence match accuracy?) is often more important than raw loss
mini_exercise: Read the abstract of 'Grokking: Generalisation Beyond Overfitting on Small Algorithmic Datasets' (Power et al., 2022). What task did they train on? What did they observe? What hyperparameter affected whether grokking occurred? Why does this matter for how you think about early stopping?
